title: Psoriasis
oneLiner: Chronic immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease with erythematous scaly plaques; common (2% UK population); associated with arthritis and cardiovascular risk.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Erythrodermic psoriasis** – diffuse erythema >90% BSA, loss of thermoregulation, high-output cardiac failure. **Key finding:** universal redness + fever + tachycardia out of proportion to skin findings.
- **Generalised pustular psoriasis (von Zumbusch)** – sterile pustules on erythematous skin, systemic toxicity, risk of sepsis and multi-organ failure. **Key finding:** sheets of non-follicular pustules + neutrophilia + fever.
- **Drug-induced exacerbation** (e.g., abrupt steroid withdrawal, lithium, beta-blockers) – can precipitate erythroderma or pustular flare. **Key finding:** temporal relationship to drug change + sudden deterioration.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale (plaque psoriasis – most common)
  - Itch (variable; less intense than eczema)
  - Nail changes: pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop discolouration
  - Joint pain/stiffness (psoriatic arthritis – 20–30%)
  - Scalp, extensor elbows/knees, lower back, and gluteal cleft predilection
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Auspitz sign (removing scale reveals pinpoint bleeding)
  - Koebner phenomenon (new lesions at sites of trauma)
  - Nail pitting or distal onycholysis
  - Joint swelling, dactylitis (sausage digit), enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Eczema (atopic dermatitis)** – ill-defined, weeping, flexural, severe pruritus; no silvery scale
- **Seborrhoeic dermatitis** – greasy yellow scale on scalp, face, chest; no nail changes
- **Lichen planus** – planar violaceous papules, Wickham striae, oral involvement; no silvery scale
- **Fungal skin infection (tinea corporis)** – annular plaque with central clearing, raised scaly border; scrapings + KOH positive
- **Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (mycosis fungoides)** – atrophic, poikilodermatous, variable scale; often truncal; biopsy shows clonal T-cells
- **Splitter:** **Silvery scale + nail pitting** → psoriasis vs. **greasy scale + no nail involvement** → seborrhoeic dermatitis
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Full skin examination (including scalp, nails, intertriginous areas)
  - Joint examination (tenderness, swelling, dactylitis)
  - Assess BSA and severity (PASI if formal scoring needed)
  - Check temperature, heart rate, blood pressure (if erythroderma/suspected pustular)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Routine:** FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP/ESR
  - **Before systemic therapy:** Hepatitis B/C, HIV, TB screening (Quantiferon or Mantoux), varicella serology (if considering biologics)
  - **If pustular/erythrodermic:** blood cultures, lactate, clotting, albumin, calcium
- **Imaging:**
  - **X-ray hands/feet** if joint symptoms (confirm psoriatic arthritis – erosions, pencil-in-cup deformity)
  - **Chest X-ray** before biologics (screen for TB)
- **Special tests:**
  - Skin biopsy (if diagnosis uncertain – shows parakeratosis, Munro microabscesses, suprapapillary thinning)
  - Dermoscopy (can help differentiate from eczema or BCC)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **PASI (Psoriasis Area and Severity Index):** 0–72; used for severity and treatment decisions. Mild <10, moderate 10–20, severe >20.
  - **DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index):** >10 on DLQI + PASI >10 often triggers systemic therapy in UK (NICE).
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy** when atypical presentation (no silvery scale, flexural only, or failure to respond) to rule out cutaneous T-cell lymphoma or other mimics.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If **erythrodermic or pustular:** admit, IV fluids (0.9% saline 1 L over 1–2 h), monitor NEWS2, blood cultures, consider IV antibiotics for superinfection if febrile. **Do NOT give systemic steroids** (risk of pustular rebound).
  - If stable plaque psoriasis: no urgent intervention needed; can manage as outpatient.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Severe erythroderma/pustular: start systemic therapy early (e.g., acitretin 25 mg PO daily, or ciclosporin 2.5 mg/kg/day PO – check local policy).
  - Topical therapy for stable plaques: emollients (50/50 liquid paraffin/white soft paraffin) + potent topical steroid (e.g., betamethasone valerate 0.1% ointment BD for 2 weeks, then step down).
  - Calcipotriol (vitamin D analogue) 50 micrograms/g cream/ointment once daily (avoid face/groin).
  - Coal tar preparations for scalp/thick plaques.
  - Consider phototherapy referral (narrowband UVB) if no contraindication.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Mild disease: emollients + topical steroids intermittent, calcipotriol.
  - Moderate-severe (PASI >10, DLQI >10): systemic therapy (methotrexate 10–25 mg weekly PO/SC + folic acid 5 mg weekly; or ciclosporin 2.5–5 mg/kg/day; or acitretin 10–25 mg/day)  
  - Biologics (adalimumab, ustekinumab, secukinumab) if failed ≥2 systemics (NICE criteria).
  - Psoriatic arthritis: rheumatology referral; consider methotrexate or biologics.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1 L over 8 h if poor oral intake/erythroderma; then oral hydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if reduced mobility (check weight/renal function).
  - **Diet:** high-calorie, high-protein if erythrodermic (catabolic state); normal diet otherwise.
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 4-hourly; if erythroderma/pustular, consider continuous monitoring.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (topical):**
  - Betamethasone valerate 0.1% cream/ointment – apply BD for up to 2 weeks (avoid >30% BSA); then reduce to OD or step to milder steroid.
  - Calcipotriol 50 micrograms/g cream/ointment – apply once daily; max 100 g/week (risk of hypercalcaemia if >100 g).
  - Combined calcipotriol/betamethasone dipropionate (Dovonex/Daivobet) – apply once daily for up to 4 weeks.
- **Systemic (first-line for moderate-severe):**
  - Methotrexate: 7.5–10 mg PO/SC weekly (increase by 2.5 mg every 2–4 weeks up to 25 mg weekly). Give folic acid 5 mg weekly (24 h after methotrexate). Check LFT, FBC, creatinine at baseline and monthly.
  - Ciclosporin: 2.5 mg/kg/day PO in two divided doses; increase to 5 mg/kg/day if no response. Monitor BP, creatinine.
  - Acitretin: 10–25 mg PO daily; teratogenic – strict contraception in women of childbearing age.
- **Biologics (second-line):**
  - Adalimumab: 80 mg SC loading, then 40 mg fortnightly (NICE criteria: failed methotrexate + ciclosporin).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Methotrexate: avoid in significant hepatic disease, renal impairment (eGFR <30), pregnancy, breastfeeding. Interacts with NSAIDs (increased toxicity) – prescribe folic acid.
  - Ciclosporin: avoid in uncontrolled hypertension, renal impairment, concurrent PUVA therapy.
  - Acitretin: absolute contraindication in pregnancy and breastfeeding; interacts with alcohol (hepatotoxicity); avoid blood donation.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Methotrexate:** reduce dose if eGFR 30–50; avoid if <30. Check LFT monthly.
  - **Ciclosporin:** reduce dose if creatinine rises >30% above baseline; avoid if eGFR <40.
  - **Acitretin:** avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Methotrexate: prescribing daily instead of weekly regimen** → life-threatening bone marrow suppression, mucositis, hepatic necrosis. Always DOUBLE CHECK the frequency. BNF states methotrexate is WEEKLY for psoriasis.
  - **Ciclosporin:** missing baseline and monitoring of creatinine and BP → irreversible nephrotoxicity.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Erythroderma – loss of barrier, hypothermia, high-output cardiac failure, sepsis.
  - Generalised pustular psoriasis – fever, leukocytosis, hypoalbuminaemia, acute kidney injury, adult respiratory distress syndrome (rare).
  - Superinfection (Staph aureus, Strep pyogenes) of eroded skin.
  - Psoriatic arthritis flare – acute joint destruction, dactylitis.
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic plaque psoriasis – psoriatic arthritis (20–30%), cardiovascular disease (increased risk, independent of risk factors), depression, social stigmatisation.
  - With systemic therapy: hepatic fibrosis (methotrexate), renal impairment (ciclosporin), infection risk (biologics).
sections.Prognosis: - Chronic relapsing course; no cure. With modern therapy, most achieve PASI 90 response. Life expectancy not significantly shortened if managed well, but cardiovascular risk is increased. Psoriatic arthritis can lead to permanent joint damage if untreated.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always screen for psoriatic arthritis** using PEST (Psoriasis Epidemiology Screening Tool) – ask about joint pain, morning stiffness, dactylitis. Refer early to rheumatology.
- **Use emollients liberally** even with topical steroids – improves barrier and reduces steroid need.
- **Check FBC, LFT, U&E before starting any systemic therapy** – and repeat monthly on methotrexate or ciclosporin.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT give systemic corticosteroids** (oral/IV) for psoriasis – risk of pustular flare and withdrawal rebound.
- **Do NOT start biologics** without checking TB screening (Quantiferon/Mantoux) – risk of reactivation.
- **Do NOT prescribe methotrexate daily** – always write “WEEKLY” on prescription chart.
- **Do NOT ignore cardiovascular risk** – check BP, lipids, and refer for lifestyle modification.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** new widespread redness (>30% BSA), fever, nausea/vomiting (dehydration), joint pain/swelling, pustules, or inability to care for self.
- **Follow-up:** Dermatology outpatient within 2–4 weeks; if on systemic, blood monitoring schedule (FBC/LFT/U&E monthly for methotrexate/ciclosporin). Provide urgent contact number (dermatology advice line or GP).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / dermatology reg if:**
  - Erythroderma or pustular psoriasis confirmed (admit)
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 or altered mental state
  - Fever >38.5°C unresponsive to paracetamol
  - Acute oliguria or rising creatinine
- **Consider ICU referral if:** high-output cardiac failure (tachycardia, hypotension, fluid overload), ARDS, or refractory metabolic acidosis.

rawMarkdown:
## Psoriasis
**One-liner:** Chronic immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease with erythematous scaly plaques; common (2% UK population); associated with arthritis and cardiovascular risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Erythrodermic psoriasis** – diffuse erythema >90% BSA, loss of thermoregulation, high-output cardiac failure. **Key finding:** universal redness + fever + tachycardia out of proportion to skin findings.
- **Generalised pustular psoriasis (von Zumbusch)** – sterile pustules on erythematous skin, systemic toxicity, risk of sepsis and multi-organ failure. **Key finding:** sheets of non-follicular pustules + neutrophilia + fever.
- **Drug-induced exacerbation** (e.g., abrupt steroid withdrawal, lithium, beta-blockers) – can precipitate erythroderma or pustular flare. **Key finding:** temporal relationship to drug change + sudden deterioration.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale (plaque psoriasis – most common)
  - Itch (variable; less intense than eczema)
  - Nail changes: pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop discolouration
  - Joint pain/stiffness (psoriatic arthritis – 20–30%)
  - Scalp, extensor elbows/knees, lower back, and gluteal cleft predilection
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Auspitz sign (removing scale reveals pinpoint bleeding)
  - Koebner phenomenon (new lesions at sites of trauma)
  - Nail pitting or distal onycholysis
  - Joint swelling, dactylitis (sausage digit), enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Eczema (atopic dermatitis)** – ill-defined, weeping, flexural, severe pruritus; no silvery scale
- **Seborrhoeic dermatitis** – greasy yellow scale on scalp, face, chest; no nail changes
- **Lichen planus** – planar violaceous papules, Wickham striae, oral involvement; no silvery scale
- **Fungal skin infection (tinea corporis)** – annular plaque with central clearing, raised scaly border; scrapings + KOH positive
- **Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (mycosis fungoides)** – atrophic, poikilodermatous, variable scale; often truncal; biopsy shows clonal T-cells
- **Splitter:** **Silvery scale + nail pitting** → psoriasis vs. **greasy scale + no nail involvement** → seborrhoeic dermatitis

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Full skin examination (including scalp, nails, intertriginous areas)
  - Joint examination (tenderness, swelling, dactylitis)
  - Assess BSA and severity (PASI if formal scoring needed)
  - Check temperature, heart rate, blood pressure (if erythroderma/suspected pustular)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Routine:** FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP/ESR
  - **Before systemic therapy:** Hepatitis B/C, HIV, TB screening (Quantiferon or Mantoux), varicella serology (if considering biologics)
  - **If pustular/erythrodermic:** blood cultures, lactate, clotting, albumin, calcium
- **Imaging:**
  - **X-ray hands/feet** if joint symptoms (confirm psoriatic arthritis – erosions, pencil-in-cup deformity)
  - **Chest X-ray** before biologics (screen for TB)
- **Special tests:**
  - Skin biopsy (if diagnosis uncertain – shows parakeratosis, Munro microabscesses, suprapapillary thinning)
  - Dermoscopy (can help differentiate from eczema or BCC)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **PASI (Psoriasis Area and Severity Index):** 0–72; used for severity and treatment decisions. Mild <10, moderate 10–20, severe >20.
  - **DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index):** >10 on DLQI + PASI >10 often triggers systemic therapy in UK (NICE).
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy** when atypical presentation (no silvery scale, flexural only, or failure to respond) to rule out cutaneous T-cell lymphoma or other mimics.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If **erythrodermic or pustular:** admit, IV fluids (0.9% saline 1 L over 1–2 h), monitor NEWS2, blood cultures, consider IV antibiotics for superinfection if febrile. **Do NOT give systemic steroids** (risk of pustular rebound).
  - If stable plaque psoriasis: no urgent intervention needed; can manage as outpatient.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Severe erythroderma/pustular: start systemic therapy early (e.g., acitretin 25 mg PO daily, or ciclosporin 2.5 mg/kg/day PO – check local policy).
  - Topical therapy for stable plaques: emollients (50/50 liquid paraffin/white soft paraffin) + potent topical steroid (e.g., betamethasone valerate 0.1% ointment BD for 2 weeks, then step down).
  - Calcipotriol (vitamin D analogue) 50 micrograms/g cream/ointment once daily (avoid face/groin).
  - Coal tar preparations for scalp/thick plaques.
  - Consider phototherapy referral (narrowband UVB) if no contraindication.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Mild disease: emollients + topical steroids intermittent, calcipotriol.
  - Moderate-severe (PASI >10, DLQI >10): systemic therapy (methotrexate 10–25 mg weekly PO/SC + folic acid 5 mg weekly; or ciclosporin 2.5–5 mg/kg/day; or acitretin 10–25 mg/day)  
  - Biologics (adalimumab, ustekinumab, secukinumab) if failed ≥2 systemics (NICE criteria).
  - Psoriatic arthritis: rheumatology referral; consider methotrexate or biologics.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1 L over 8 h if poor oral intake/erythroderma; then oral hydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if reduced mobility (check weight/renal function).
  - **Diet:** high-calorie, high-protein if erythrodermic (catabolic state); normal diet otherwise.
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 4-hourly; if erythroderma/pustular, consider continuous monitoring.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (topical):**
  - Betamethasone valerate 0.1% cream/ointment – apply BD for up to 2 weeks (avoid >30% BSA); then reduce to OD or step to milder steroid.
  - Calcipotriol 50 micrograms/g cream/ointment – apply once daily; max 100 g/week (risk of hypercalcaemia if >100 g).
  - Combined calcipotriol/betamethasone dipropionate (Dovonex/Daivobet) – apply once daily for up to 4 weeks.
- **Systemic (first-line for moderate-severe):**
  - Methotrexate: 7.5–10 mg PO/SC weekly (increase by 2.5 mg every 2–4 weeks up to 25 mg weekly). Give folic acid 5 mg weekly (24 h after methotrexate). Check LFT, FBC, creatinine at baseline and monthly.
  - Ciclosporin: 2.5 mg/kg/day PO in two divided doses; increase to 5 mg/kg/day if no response. Monitor BP, creatinine.
  - Acitretin: 10–25 mg PO daily; teratogenic – strict contraception in women of childbearing age.
- **Biologics (second-line):**
  - Adalimumab: 80 mg SC loading, then 40 mg fortnightly (NICE criteria: failed methotrexate + ciclosporin).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Methotrexate: avoid in significant hepatic disease, renal impairment (eGFR <30), pregnancy, breastfeeding. Interacts with NSAIDs (increased toxicity) – prescribe folic acid.
  - Ciclosporin: avoid in uncontrolled hypertension, renal impairment, concurrent PUVA therapy.
  - Acitretin: absolute contraindication in pregnancy and breastfeeding; interacts with alcohol (hepatotoxicity); avoid blood donation.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Methotrexate:** reduce dose if eGFR 30–50; avoid if <30. Check LFT monthly.
  - **Ciclosporin:** reduce dose if creatinine rises >30% above baseline; avoid if eGFR <40.
  - **Acitretin:** avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Methotrexate: prescribing daily instead of weekly regimen** → life-threatening bone marrow suppression, mucositis, hepatic necrosis. Always DOUBLE CHECK the frequency. BNF states methotrexate is WEEKLY for psoriasis.
  - **Ciclosporin:** missing baseline and monitoring of creatinine and BP → irreversible nephrotoxicity.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Erythroderma – loss of barrier, hypothermia, high-output cardiac failure, sepsis.
  - Generalised pustular psoriasis – fever, leukocytosis, hypoalbuminaemia, acute kidney injury, adult respiratory distress syndrome (rare).
  - Superinfection (Staph aureus, Strep pyogenes) of eroded skin.
  - Psoriatic arthritis flare – acute joint destruction, dactylitis.
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic plaque psoriasis – psoriatic arthritis (20–30%), cardiovascular disease (increased risk, independent of risk factors), depression, social stigmatisation.
  - With systemic therapy: hepatic fibrosis (methotrexate), renal impairment (ciclosporin), infection risk (biologics).

### Prognosis
- Chronic relapsing course; no cure. With modern therapy, most achieve PASI 90 response. Life expectancy not significantly shortened if managed well, but cardiovascular risk is increased. Psoriatic arthritis can lead to permanent joint damage if untreated.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always screen for psoriatic arthritis** using PEST (Psoriasis Epidemiology Screening Tool) – ask about joint pain, morning stiffness, dactylitis. Refer early to rheumatology.
- **Use emollients liberally** even with topical steroids – improves barrier and reduces steroid need.
- **Check FBC, LFT, U&E before starting any systemic therapy** – and repeat monthly on methotrexate or ciclosporin.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT give systemic corticosteroids** (oral/IV) for psoriasis – risk of pustular flare and withdrawal rebound.
- **Do NOT start biologics** without checking TB screening (Quantiferon/Mantoux) – risk of reactivation.
- **Do NOT prescribe methotrexate daily** – always write “WEEKLY” on prescription chart.
- **Do NOT ignore cardiovascular risk** – check BP, lipids, and refer for lifestyle modification.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** new widespread redness (>30% BSA), fever, nausea/vomiting (dehydration), joint pain/swelling, pustules, or inability to care for self.
- **Follow-up:** Dermatology outpatient within 2–4 weeks; if on systemic, blood monitoring schedule (FBC/LFT/U&E monthly for methotrexate/ciclosporin). Provide urgent contact number (dermatology advice line or GP).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / dermatology reg if:**
  - Erythroderma or pustular psoriasis confirmed (admit)
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 or altered mental state
  - Fever >38.5°C unresponsive to paracetamol
  - Acute oliguria or rising creatinine
- **Consider ICU referral if:** high-output cardiac failure (tachycardia, hypotension, fluid overload), ARDS, or refractory metabolic acidosis.
